title: Dog Swallowed Sugar-Free Gum with Xylitol: Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Xylitol ingestion is a life-threatening emergency for dogs. Learn what to do and why immediate veterinary care is essential to save your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Xylitol is a highly toxic sugar substitute that causes life-threatening low blood sugar and liver failure in dogs; immediate emergency veterinary care is required if ingested.

What is xylitol and why is it dangerous for my dog?

Xylitol is a sugar substitute commonly used in sugar-free gum, breath mints, and some peanut butters. While it is safe for human consumption, it is extremely toxic to dogs because it triggers a rapid and massive release of insulin.

Is xylitol ingestion considered a veterinary emergency for my dog?

  • Yes, this is a High-Urgency Emergency.
  • Ingestion can cause life-threatening hypoglycemia (dangerously low blood sugar) within 30 minutes.
  • It can lead to acute liver failure or even death.
  • If your dog has swallowed gum containing xylitol, you must contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control center immediately.

How does having a photo of the product packaging help the veterinarian?

  • Taking a quick photo of the gum packaging or bringing the wrapper with you to the clinic is incredibly helpful for the triage team.
  • Different brands contain different concentrations of xylitol, which determines the severity of the case.
  • The ingredient list allows the veterinarian to calculate the exact toxic dose based on your dog's weight.
  • This calculation helps determine the most effective treatment plan and prognosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, xylitol ingestion stimulates a rapid, dose-dependent insulin release, potentially causing profound hypoglycemia at dosages exceeding 75-100 mg/kg. Some dogs ingesting xylitol at dosages >500 mg/kg may develop severe hepatic insufficiency or failure. Hypoglycemia can manifest within 30 minutes of ingestion or be delayed up to 12-18 hours, presenting as vomiting, weakness, ataxia, depression, hypokalemia, seizures, and coma. Liver injury signs may appear ≥24-48 hours post-ingestion, though liver enzyme increases can be detected within 8-12 hours. Emesis should only be attempted under veterinary supervision in asymptomatic animals, as activated charcoal is ineffective for xylitol. Animals ingesting >75-100 mg/kg should be hospitalized with baseline blood glucose monitoring every 1-2 hours for at least 12 hours. Animals ingesting >500 mg/kg should also have baseline liver values measured and monitored every 24 hours for at least 72 hours. Hypoglycemia is managed with IV dextrose boluses and/or constant-rate infusions, continuing until the dog maintains normal blood glucose without supplementation. Dextrose and hepatoprotectants (N-acetylcysteine, S-adenosylmethionine, silymarin) should be considered for dogs ingesting xylitol at >500 mg/kg, even if normoglycemic.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: What are the early symptoms of xylitol poisoning?
faqs.answer: Initial signs include vomiting, followed by symptoms of low blood sugar such as lethargy, stumbling, weakness, collapses, or seizures.

faqs.question: Can I wait to see if my dog acts sick?
faqs.answer: No. By the time symptoms appear, significant internal damage may have already occurred. Immediate intervention is required to prevent liver failure.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless a veterinarian specifically tells you to do so, as this can be dangerous if the dog is already becoming weak or disoriented.
